title: TPU v1 · The Systolic Array as a 256-Stage 2D Pipeline
oneLine: A systolic array is fundamentally a two-dimensional pipeline: data advances by one PE per cycle, making the TPU a textbook example of trading latency for throughput.

legend.name: PE = one pipeline stage
legend.desc: Each PE performs only 1 multiply and 1 accumulate per cycle. The shallow logic keeps stage delay low enough to support 700 MHz.

legend.name: Inter-PE pipeline registers
legend.desc: Staging registers between adjacent PEs latch data every cycle and break a long combinational path into short stages—the physical mechanism that makes the design a pipeline.

legend.name: Activation input (from left)
legend.desc: Activations enter from the left and advance one stage per cycle like a propagating wave.

legend.name: Partial-sum output (downward)
legend.desc: Partial sums accumulate down each column and exit at the bottom.

steps.cap: ① Startup / fill: during the first few cycles, only a few PEs near the upper-left corner are active as the pipeline fills.
② Filling: the activation wave advances diagonally and progressively engages more PEs.
③ Full pipeline: the entire array computes, producing one complete result column per cycle (throughput = 1 column/cycle).
④ Latency vs. throughput: one input must traverse a full PE column before producing a result (latency ≈ array depth, 256 cycles), while steady state emits one column per cycle.
⑤ Drain: the final wave exits and the pipeline empties. Large matrices and batches amortize fill and drain overhead.

before: Define the question first: Should this block be pipelined, and into how many stages? The answer is fundamentally set by target frequency versus critical-path delay. A systolic array naturally pipelines matrix multiplication across rows of PEs.
Decisions to make: Pipeline depth; logic per stage (stage-delay/logic-depth budget); staging registers between PEs; and whether hazard handling such as forwarding or stalls is required.
Required inputs: The target frequency and throughput from architecture (③), an estimate of the block’s critical path, and whether the workload is latency-sensitive or throughput-sensitive.
Trade-offs to resolve: Latency versus throughput; whether the workload can amortize fill/drain overhead; and whether data or control dependencies require forwarding, stalls, or branch prediction.
during: Goal: Increase throughput by overlapping execution. The systolic array spreads a large matrix multiply across rows of PEs; data advances one stage per cycle and each stage performs a small amount of work.

Choose stage count/depth: Each PE performs only 1 8-bit multiply and 1 accumulate, so its logic is shallow and stage delay is low enough for 700 MHz. Pipeline depth equals the array dimension (256), chosen together with throughput and operand-supply bandwidth.

Natural balancing: Every PE is identical and performs the same work each cycle, so there is no unusually slow stage. This is far easier to balance than a general-purpose CPU pipeline with IF/ID/EX/MEM/WB stages.

Eliminate conventional hazards: The dataflow is static and deterministic, with no branches or writeback dependencies, so it does not need the full forwarding/stall/branch-prediction machinery. TPU spends that area on MACs instead.

Latency is the cost: An input must traverse a full PE column before producing a result (≈256 cycles), but once full the array emits one column per cycle. Large matrices and batches therefore amortize fill and drain.
after: Run a synthesis timing trial: Pass the pipeline’s critical path through quick logic synthesis to confirm 700 MHz. If it misses, add a pipeline stage or retime it; changing stage count changes cycle-level behavior and requires renewed verification.
Build a cycle model: Confirm target utilization at full pipeline and acceptable fill/drain overhead for target matrix sizes. Otherwise revisit array size or batching strategy.
Define the block verification plan: Cover and stimulate pipeline hazards and boundaries, including fill, full operation, drain, and backpressure.
Hand off to RTL (⑤): Implement every pipeline stage, staging register, and control FSM as synthesizable Verilog.
Reserve low-power hooks: During decode, PEs are often idle. Provide fine-grained clock gating so idle units do not consume dynamic power.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017. Facts disclosed in the paper are distinguished from illustrative examples and standard industry practice; the latter are not presented as disclosed TPU v1 implementation details.
